Bedouin qaf reflex [ɡ], emphatic /lˤ/ and epenthetic [ʊ] (u-colouring). Source cites Jastrow (2006).
Source cites Jastrow (2006:416).
Kaf affrication WITHOUT an adjacent front vowel, plus cluster-splitting epenthesis. Contradicts the front-vowel conditioning of o2i's IQ_GILIT_AFFRIC_K_* rules.
Affrication plus /a/ > [i] raising in the open syllable.
Geminate kaf affricates as a geminate affricate.
Word-final affrication after a LONG BACK vowel — again not front-vowel-conditioned.

The source gives BOTH [simatʃ] (p.23) and [simaʃ] (p.25) for the same word: gilit kaf has a fricated [ʃ] and an affricated [tʃ] reflex. The affricate is recorded here; the fricative variant is an in-source disagreement, not an error.
/a/ > [ʊ] in a post-velar (emphatic) environment.
u-colouring and vowel harmony around labials/velars.
Emphatic /rˤ/ (Younis 1994; Youssef 2009 as cited).
Monophthongization of OA /aj/.
Attributed by the thesis to Mahdi (1985), who describes the gilit of Basra rather than of Baghdad.
Qaf retained — the defining qəltu reflex, against gilit [ɡaːm].
Rightward emphasis harmony: /s/ > [sˤ]; /uː/ lowered to [oː] in the post-velar context.
Velar /k/ preserved — no affrication in qəltu. Minimal contrast with jasim2020-007.
Contrasts with gilit [tʃaːn] (same page).

OA /r/ > [ɣ] in Muslawi Qəltu (Tawfiq 2010; Jastrow 2006:416 as cited). The source's phonemic form /baɾid/ has a short first vowel; the standard spelling بارد has a long one — orthography editor-supplied, hence medium confidence.
/r/ > [ɣ] in a long-vowel environment.
/r/ > [ɣ]; interdental /θ/ retained.
Monophthongization of OA /aj/ (attributed to Jastrow 1994).
Monophthongization of OA /aw/.
Qəltu schwa for OA /i, u/ plus cluster-splitting epenthesis. The source omits the initial glottal stop.
The source omits the initial glottal stop that أ writes.
Table (6). The ar-JO spec cites this example as 'Fadda 2016:30'; the table is on printed page 29.
Emphasis spreads rightwards onto the final /t/ — the source writes [sˤoːtˤ], not [sˤoːt].
Diphthong retained before a glide.
SA /u/ realized [ʊ] or [ɵ] in Ammani.
Contrasted with neighbouring Levantine (incl. Jerusalem) [ʔɪmm].
Minimal pair with [ħɪbb] 'love (verb)'.
Minimal pair with [ħubb]; the two are distinguished only by the ḥaraka, so the vocalized orthography is what carries the contrast.
Word-final imāla: SA /a/ (tā marbūṭa) > [ɛ].
Blocking of final imāla after a pharyngeal — the counterpart of fadda2016-008.

The source gives FOUR co-existing realizations of the stressed /aː/ (generational/origin-conditioned). The most conservative is recorded; any single-IPA gold row here is a simplification of what the source actually claims.

source_variety: 'bi'ul' group — urban Palestinian type, the base of Western Amman speech (Cleveland 1963 as cited)
notes: /ð/ > [z] plus nasal place assimilation to the following labial. Variety label is the source's ('bi'ul' group), so this row could equally be tagged ar-PS.
/θ/ > [t] (interdental merger to a stop).
/ðˤ/ > [zˤ]. Note the source's own SA form has a schwa nucleus.
Historic /ð/ realized as the stop [d] in the modern dialect. Normalization applied: Arabicist ⟨d, a, h, b⟩ → IPA [d, a, h, b] (identity).

One of the FEW words keeping a true interdental [θ] — and the source says it does so only because it is a formal-register MSA borrowing, not because the dialect has /θ/. Normalization: ā→aː, y→j, doubled letters→geminates.
Interdental retained in an MSA borrowing. Normalization: ḥ→ħ, ī→iː.

source_variety: Gaza City Arabic (Salonen's 1979/1980 texts, as reported)
notes: Reported from Salonen's texts, which use ⟨ḏ⟩ for the historic phoneme while the same paragraph argues the Gaza realization is the stop [d]. The IPA here follows the paper's argument (stop reflex), NOT a literal transliteration of ⟨ḏ⟩ — hence low confidence.

source_variety: CI — central-interior subdialect (Castelo Branco / Beira Baixa)
notes: Palatalization of stressed /u/ > [y]. Notation: the source marks stress with an apostrophe AFTER the onset ([l'y…]); normalized to the IPA stress mark before the syllable (ˈly…).
Stressed /u/ > [y] AND deletion of the final unstressed [u] (feature 1.5).
Palatalization of stressed [a] > [ɛ] after a high vowel in the preceding syllable; final [u] reduced to [ɨ].
[a] > [ɛ] after a glide.
The [o] from monophthongized ⟨ou⟩ palatalizes to [ø]; final unstressed vowel deleted.
The source gives two variants; the first is recorded.
Stressed [e] rounded to [œ] (feature 1.4) — not modelled by o2i's pt-PT-x-beira spec.
[e] > [œ] plus final-vowel deletion.

source_variety: SW — southwestern subdialect (Baixo Alentejo / Alentejo littoral)
notes: Chain shift: [e] > [ɛ].
Chain shift: [ɛ] > [æ].
Stressed [a] backed; the prose says 'almost as [ɔ]' while the example is transcribed [ɒ] — the transcription is recorded.
[ɔ] raised towards [o] (raising diacritic kept).
Stressed /u/ > [y] — the isogloss shared with CI. Note the SW form has a spirantized final [ð] where CI has [d] (brissos2014-002).

Minimal pair with galˤlˤab illustrating that Nigerian Arabic has phonemic /lˤ/ beyond the four Classical emphatics (Owens 2006 p.25). qaf->g. Arabic spelling editor-supplied; source prints romanized only.
Minimal pair with gallab: phonemic emphatic /lˤ/ (Owens 2006 p.25). No standard Arabic spelling supplied (distinct root, emphasis not orthographically marked).
Minimal pair with karˤrˤa: phonemic emphatic /rˤ/ (Owens 2006 p.25).
Minimal pair with karra: phonemic emphatic /rˤ/ (Owens 2006 p.25).

Minimal pair with amˤmˤ: phonemic emphatic /mˤ/ (Owens 2006 p.25). Recorded exactly as printed: Owens writes no pharyngeal onset for etymological ʕ (عمّ), so the ipa follows the printed form and is NOT a claim that Nigerian Arabic drops /ʕ/ here.
Minimal pair with amm: phonemic emphatic /mˤ/, and the vowel is /a/ not /u/ (Owens 2006 p.25). Recorded as printed (no glottal onset written for etymological ʔ).
Gahawa syndrome: /a/ inserted after the guttural, ahamar < *ʔaḥmar (Owens 2006 p.25). The medial h is the reflex of etymological ḥ, normalized to /ħ/; the initial ʔ is not written by the source.

3sg m perfect; contrasts with the 1sg (final-stressed, -t lost) in Owens' argument for an underlying suffix (Owens 2006 pp.13-14). Confidence capped at medium: transliterated (non-IPA) source notation.

Gahawa type 2a (CaGC# > CaGaC#): /a/ inserted after the velar guttural (Prochazka 2026 p.318, Table 5.2.1, Nigeria row). Source writes x; o2i uses x for خ, so no change. No standard Arabic spelling supplied.

The plain /ž/ [ʒ] reflex of Old Arabic *ǧ, the pan-Maghrebi default; short vowels absent → CC onset. (The gemination allophone d-ǧbəl 'the mountain' shows the affricate surfacing when geminate.) Normalization: ž→ʒ.

Affricate /ǧ/ [dʒ] RETAINED word-initially in a Moroccan pre-Hilali pocket (Behnstedt & Benabbou 2002:56-57) — evidence the affricate is not lost group-wide, so /dʒ/ stays a secondary ǧīm candidate. Normalization: ǧ→dʒ, ī→iː.
Affricate /ǧ/ [dʒ] retained word-internally in the same pre-Hilali pocket (Behnstedt & Benabbou 2002:56-57). Normalization: ǧ→dʒ.
ǧīm as geminate fricative /ʒʒ/; the source lists ḥāžža~ḥāzza, the ~ḥāzza variant showing the further *ǧ>ž>z sibilant shift of the old medina speech. Normalization: ḥ→ħ, ā→aː, ž→ʒ, doubled→geminate.

Long-distance SIBILANT HARMONY: the /ž/ reflex of *ǧ (in *zawǧ) assimilates to the stem's /z/, giving zōz (Singer 1984:9). A conditioned Tunisian/Libyan shift, left to the leaves — the node keeps the /ʒ/ default. Normalization: ō→oː.
Same sibilant-harmony reflex of *zawǧ in Libyan Tripoli (Pereira 2010:66-67); Guerrero calls the ž→z harmony a distinguishing feature of most Tunisian and Libyan dialects. Normalization: ō→oː.
*ʕaǧūz → ʕzūz: the *ǧ→ž reflex harmonised to /z/ before the stem sibilant, plus Maghrebi first-vowel syncope (Pereira 2010:66-67). Normalization: ū→uː.
Regressive dissimilation of chuintants: *ǧ (before the stem sibilant /z/) surfaces as the stop /d/ in Tunis (*ǧazīra → dzīra). A conditioned Tunis reflex, leaf territory. Normalization: ī→iː.
DEVOICING: /ž/ (<*ǧ) has the allophone [ʃ] before a voiceless consonant in Tripoli and Ḥassāniyya (Pereira 2010:63). Source gives the phonetic [ʃħuːʃ]. Normalization: ū→uː.
Same word-medial devoicing of /ž/ to [ʃ] before voiceless /t/ (W. Marçais 1908:16, as cited by Guerrero). Source gives ['zuːʃtah]; stress dropped. Normalization: ū→uː.

p.200: Maghrebi varieties disallow vowels in light syllables, so *katab > Tripoli ktǝb (schwa/short-vowel deletion; ǝ = [ə]). Vocalization editor-supplied to feed o2i's diacritized-input contract; the printed form is romanized.
p.200: contrasted with Tripoli ktǝb — Benghazi (eastern Libyan) DOES allow the light syllable, keeping short /i/. Vocalization editor-supplied.
p.201: Italian loan grigio, showing the contact phonemes /v, č, ǧ/ borrowed into Libyan Arabic (here ǧ = affricate [dʒ]). A loanword with an editor-supplied Arabic spelling, hence low confidence.
